visualStrategies.strategy: Clinical authority — white background, oversized certificate badges, lab coat imagery. Communicates pharmaceutical-grade trust without claiming it. Every image contains one claim and one supporting credential.
visualStrategies.hookLine: Tested by science. Trusted by doctors.

visualStrategies.strategy: Outcome-first lifestyle — lead image is a sleeping woman, not a bottle. The product appears in image 3. Converts by selling the benefit before the product, which reduces the cognitive cost of evaluating the category.

Value density — comparison table as hero image showing milligrams per dollar vs every named competitor. Anchors the price objection before it can form. Works well in a category where price sensitivity is high.

Minimalist premium — all-white images with a single product and one bold benefit per slide. Eight images, eight benefits, no clutter. Converts on simplicity and confidence. The restraint signals quality.

Community trust — every image features a customer quote in large mono font. No stock photos, no infographics. Converts on social proof density. Effective because supplement buyers are skeptical of brand claims and responsive to peer testimony.

gapAnalysis: The category is split between clinical authority (competitor 1) and outcome-first lifestyle (competitor 2). Doctor's Best sits in neither lane clearly — the current listing uses clinical language but lifestyle-adjacent imagery. This mixed signal reduces conviction at the point of decision. The glycinate bioavailability advantage (up to 80% absorbed vs 4% for oxide) is the strongest differentiator in the category and no competitor is currently leading with it as a standalone visual claim.
opening: Own the bioavailability argument. No competitor is using the absorption comparison as a first-image hook. A hero image built around '80% absorbed vs 4% in standard magnesium' with the TRAACS badge occupies unclaimed high-intent search real estate and directly addresses the most common purchase objection: why is this more expensive than the $8 bottle?
